questions.stem: A 28-year-old vegetarian has fatigue and a microcytic anemia. Her physician wants the single best test of body iron stores. Which test should be ordered?
questions.explain: Ferritin is the best single test of iron stores; a low value is essentially diagnostic of iron deficiency (remember it is also an acute-phase reactant).

guide.s: best single test for iron stores

questions.stem: Blood passing through exercising skeletal muscle releases more oxygen to the tissue. Which change shifts the oxygen–hemoglobin dissociation curve in the direction responsible for this?
questions.explain: Active tissue generates CO₂ and H⁺, lowering hemoglobin affinity and shifting the curve right (the Bohr effect) to unload more O₂.

questions.stem: In an emergency with no time for full cross-match, which red blood cell group can be given to a recipient of any ABO type?
options: Only a perfect ABO match is ever acceptable
questions.explain: Group O RBCs lack A and B antigens, so they are the universal RBC donor. (Group AB is the universal RBC recipient.)

questions.stem: Which bleeding feature most suggests a platelet / primary hemostasis problem rather than a coagulation factor defect?
questions.explain: Petechiae and mucosal bleeding (epistaxis, gum bleeding, menorrhagia) are the primary-hemostasis pattern; deep tissue bleeds and hemarthroses suggest a coagulation-factor problem.

questions.stem: Fetal hemoglobin (HbF) differs from adult HbA in its globin chain composition, giving it higher oxygen affinity. What is the composition of HbF?
questions.explain: HbF = 2 alpha + 2 gamma chains. Its higher O₂ affinity lets the fetus extract oxygen from maternal blood across the placenta.

questions.stem: A macrocytic anemia shows macro-ovalocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils on the film. This morphology indicates which process?
questions.explain: Macro-ovalocytes with hypersegmented neutrophils are the hallmark of megaloblastic anemia from impaired DNA synthesis (B12 or folate deficiency).

questions.stem: A well 24-year-old man has Hb 122 g/L, MCV 67 fL, a relatively high RBC count, normal RDW, and normal ferritin; the film shows target cells. Which test best confirms the likely diagnosis?
questions.explain: Marked microcytosis with preserved Hb, normal RDW, normal ferritin and target cells points to thalassemia trait; β-trait is confirmed by elevated HbA₂ on electrophoresis/HPLC. Do not give iron without proven deficiency.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman has thrombocytopenia, anemia with schistocytes, confusion, mild renal impairment and fever. Coagulation studies are normal. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Reassure and repeat CBC in a week
questions.explain: Thrombocytopenia + microangiopathic hemolysis + neuro/renal findings with normal coagulation is TTP — a medical emergency treated with plasma exchange. Avoid platelet transfusion unless life-threatening bleeding.

questions.stem: An 78-year-old woman with heart failure becomes dyspneic 2 hours into her second unit of RBCs. She is hypertensive with raised JVP, crackles, and a high BNP, and improves with furosemide. Which reaction is this?
questions.explain: Volume overload with hypertension, raised JVP, pulmonary edema, high BNP and a diuretic response is TACO. TRALI is non-cardiogenic and is not volume-overload driven.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man develops acute hypoxemia and bilateral pulmonary infiltrates within 4 hours of a plasma transfusion. He is normotensive with normal JVP and a normal BNP. Which reaction is most likely?
questions.explain: Acute hypoxemia with bilateral infiltrates soon after transfusion, without volume overload (normal BP/JVP/BNP), is TRALI — non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema from donor antibodies.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old with sickle cell disease has chest pain, fever, hypoxia and a new pulmonary infiltrate. Besides analgesia and oxygen, which is a key part of management of this complication?
questions.explain: This is acute chest syndrome, an emergency: oxygen, antibiotics, analgesia, incentive spirometry, and (exchange) transfusion in severe cases.

questions.stem: Why do patients with sickle cell disease need pneumococcal vaccination and, in childhood, penicillin prophylaxis?
options: Functional asplenia raises the risk of encapsulated-organism infection
questions.explain: Repeated splenic infarction causes functional asplenia, raising the risk of infection with encapsulated organisms — hence vaccination and penicillin prophylaxis.

questions.stem: A patient has a megaloblastic anemia, but serum B12 and folate are both borderline. Which test best distinguishes clinically important B12 deficiency, which must not be missed?
questions.explain: Methylmalonic acid is elevated in B12 deficiency but not folate deficiency. Identifying B12 deficiency matters because giving folate alone can let neurologic injury progress.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with heavy menses has Hb 96 g/L, MCV 71 fL, high RDW, low ferritin, and a platelet count of 580 × 10⁹/L. How should the elevated platelet count be interpreted?
questions.explain: Iron deficiency commonly causes a reactive thrombocytosis; the count typically normalizes once iron is replaced. No cytoreduction is needed.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old has pancytopenia with no splenomegaly and no blasts; the marrow is markedly hypocellular. Reticulocytes are low. Which diagnosis best fits?
questions.explain: A hypocellular marrow with pancytopenia, low reticulocytes and no infiltrating cells or splenomegaly is characteristic of aplastic anemia (marrow failure).

questions.stem: A patient has anemia with high reticulocytes, high LDH, high indirect bilirubin, low haptoglobin and spherocytes on film. Which test best separates autoimmune hemolytic anemia from hereditary spherocytosis?
questions.explain: Both show spherocytes and hemolysis. A positive DAT indicates autoimmune hemolysis; hereditary spherocytosis is DAT-negative.

questions.stem: An RhD-negative woman with no anti-D antibodies is pregnant with an RhD-positive fetus. How does RhD immune globulin prevent hemolytic disease of the fetus/newborn in future pregnancies?
options: It passively clears fetal D-positive cells before the mother mounts an anti-D response
It replaces a missing maternal clotting factor
questions.explain: RhD immune globulin (passive anti-D) clears fetal RhD-positive RBCs before the maternal immune system is sensitized, preventing anti-D alloimmunization that would threaten a later D-positive pregnancy.

questions.stem: A woman develops massive postpartum hemorrhage. She is oozing from venipuncture sites; platelets are 60 × 10⁹/L, PT and PTT are prolonged, fibrinogen is very low, D-dimer is high, and schistocytes are present. Beyond treating the obstetric cause, which product most directly corrects the key deficiency here?
questions.explain: This is DIC with consumptive hypofibrinogenemia. Alongside treating the cause, cryoprecipitate/fibrinogen concentrate replaces fibrinogen (with FFP/platelets as guided). Definitive control requires stopping the obstetric bleeding.

questions.stem: A man rescued from a house fire is confused. His pulse oximeter reads 98%, yet he is hypoxic at the tissue level. Which statement best explains the oxygen-delivery problem?
options: CO binds hemoglobin and shifts the curve right, improving unloading
CO binds hemoglobin, shifts the curve left, and pulse oximetry reads falsely normal
Methemoglobin lowers affinity and unloads more O₂
Fetal hemoglobin is being produced acutely
questions.explain: Carbon monoxide binds hemoglobin avidly, shifts the curve left (impairing unloading), and is not distinguished from O₂ by standard pulse oximetry, giving a falsely normal reading. Measure carboxyhemoglobin.

guide.s: carbon monoxide left shift false oximetry

questions.stem: A previously transfused man is readmitted 8 days after an uneventful RBC transfusion with falling hemoglobin, mild jaundice and a newly positive direct antiglobulin test; the original cross-match was compatible. Which reaction explains this?
questions.explain: An anamnestic rise in a non-ABO alloantibody (e.g. Kidd) days to weeks later causes a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction: falling Hb, jaundice and a newly positive DAT despite an initially compatible cross-match.

cards.prompt: What are the first two questions to ask about any abnormal CBC?
cards.answer: Which cell line(s) are abnormal, and is it one line or several? Multiple lines down points to a marrow (factory) problem until proven otherwise.

cards.text: By MCV, anemia is microcytic if MCV {{<80}} fL, normocytic {{80–100}} fL, and macrocytic if {{>100}} fL.
cards.explain: MCV is the first branch point of the anemia algorithm.

cards.prompt: What two tests form the backbone of the anemia algorithm?
cards.answer: The MCV (size) and the reticulocyte count (production vs loss/destruction).

cards.prompt: A high RDW with microcytosis favours which diagnosis over thalassemia trait?
cards.explain: Iron deficiency produces variably sized cells (high RDW); thalassemia trait cells are uniformly small (normal RDW).

cards.prompt: Match the reticulocyte response to its meaning:
pairs: Underproduction: deficiency, renal, inflammation, marrow failure
cards.explain: Reticulocytes separate loss/destruction from a production problem.

cards.prompt: Which physical sign is most directly attributable to anemia itself?
cards.answer: Pallor, especially conjunctival pallor, from reduced hemoglobin/oxygen-carrying capacity.

cards.prompt: Match each red-cell finding to its association:
cards.explain: Film morphology often points straight to the mechanism.

cards.prompt: Which two film findings are 'never-miss', urgent results?
cards.explain: Schistocytes (thrombotic microangiopathy) and blasts (acute leukemia) both demand urgent action.

cards.prompt: Basophilia on the differential should raise suspicion of which group of disorders?
cards.answer: Myeloproliferative neoplasms, especially chronic myeloid leukemia (CML).

cards.text: A low platelet count caused by {{clumping}} in the EDTA tube is called {{pseudothrombocytopenia}} and must be excluded before working up true thrombocytopenia.
cards.explain: Always confirm a low platelet count and review the film for clumps.

cards.prompt: Match each factor to the curve shift it causes:
cards.explain: Right shift unloads O₂ to active tissue; left shift holds onto it.

cards.text: Adult HbA is made of 2 {{alpha}} and 2 {{beta}} chains, each with a heme, so one hemoglobin binds up to {{4}} oxygen molecules.
cards.explain: Four hemes per tetramer; binding is cooperative (sigmoid curve).

cards.prompt: What is the Bohr effect?
cards.answer: In active tissue, rising CO₂ and H⁺ lower hemoglobin's O₂ affinity (right shift), promoting oxygen unloading where it is needed.

cards.prompt: Carbon monoxide poisoning classically produces which oxygen-delivery picture?
options: Left shift with a falsely normal SpO₂
cards.explain: CO shifts the curve left and is read as oxyhemoglobin by standard pulse oximetry, giving a falsely normal SpO₂.

cards.prompt: Match the life stage to its main hematopoietic site:
cards.explain: Hematopoiesis migrates from yolk sac to liver to axial marrow.

cards.prompt: What does pancytopenia usually imply about the cause?
cards.answer: All three lines down points to a bone-marrow (factory) problem — failure, infiltration/replacement, or ineffective hematopoiesis — until proven otherwise.

cards.prompt: Match the pancytopenia mechanism to its example:
cards.explain: Categorize, then test for reversible causes and consider marrow biopsy.

cards.prompt: Which findings in pancytopenia are urgent red flags? (select all)
cards.explain: Febrile neutropenia, very low platelets/bleeding and blasts all demand urgent action.

cards.prompt: Match each iron study to its value in iron deficiency:
cards.explain: Low ferritin + low TSAT + high TIBC is the classic iron-deficiency pattern.

cards.text: Low ferritin is diagnostic of iron deficiency, but ferritin is an {{acute-phase reactant}}, so a {{normal/high}} value does not exclude deficiency during inflammation.
cards.explain: In inflammation, confirm with transferrin saturation/TIBC.

cards.prompt: A man or postmenopausal woman has new iron-deficiency anemia. What must be excluded?
cards.answer: GI blood loss, including malignancy, until proven otherwise — evaluate the GI tract.

cards.prompt: Which is a clear indication for IV rather than oral iron?
options: Mild iron deficiency tolerating oral iron
cards.explain: IV iron is used for intolerance, malabsorption, ongoing loss beyond oral replacement, or when rapid repletion is needed.

cards.text: A craving for ice is called {{pagophagia}} and is a classic symptom of {{iron deficiency}}.
cards.explain: Pica/pagophagia, restless legs and koilonychia are iron-deficiency clues.

cards.prompt: Why must you avoid empiric iron in microcytosis due to thalassemia?
cards.answer: Thalassemia microcytosis is from reduced globin production, not iron lack; giving iron without proven deficiency risks iron overload.

cards.text: β-thalassemia trait typically shows a very low MCV with a {{normal}} RDW and an elevated {{HbA₂}} on electrophoresis.
cards.explain: Contrast with iron deficiency: high RDW, low ferritin, normal electrophoresis.

cards.prompt: Match each sickle cell complication to a key point:
pairs: Prompt analgesia, hydration, O₂ only if hypoxic
cards.explain: Vaso-occlusion and hemolysis drive the clinical picture.

cards.prompt: How does hydroxyurea modify sickle cell disease, and what must be monitored?
cards.answer: It increases fetal hemoglobin (HbF), reducing vaso-occlusive episodes; monitor the CBC for myelosuppression.

cards.prompt: Which set of labs indicates active hemolysis?
options: High reticulocytes, high LDH, high indirect bilirubin, low haptoglobin
Low reticulocytes, low LDH, high haptoglobin
Low retics, high ferritin, normal LDH
cards.explain: Hemolysis: reticulocytosis, high LDH and indirect bilirubin, low haptoglobin.

cards.prompt: Match the cause of thrombocytopenia to its clue:
pairs: Isolated low platelets, otherwise normal CBC/film
Low fibrinogen, high D-dimer, prolonged PT/PTT
cards.explain: Coagulation studies and the film separate these quickly.

cards.prompt: Order these platelet counts from least to most dangerous (lowest bleeding risk first):
cards.explain: Risk rises as the count falls; <10 carries serious spontaneous bleeding risk.

cards.prompt: Which is true of platelet transfusion in TTP?
cards.explain: In TTP, plasma exchange is the treatment; platelet transfusion is generally avoided unless there is life-threatening bleeding.

cards.prompt: How is immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) managed across severities?
cards.answer: Observe mild, isolated cases; use steroids or IVIG for severe bleeding or very low counts.

cards.text: Thrombocytopenia plus {{schistocytes}} is an emergency until {{TTP/DIC/HUS}} is excluded.
cards.explain: Microangiopathy with low platelets is a never-miss combination.

cards.prompt: Match each ABO group to its plasma antibodies:
cards.explain: Antibodies are against the antigens the person lacks.

cards.prompt: What is the mechanism of an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction from ABO mismatch?
cards.answer: Recipient naturally occurring IgM binds donor RBC ABO antigens, activates complement, and causes intravascular hemolysis.

cards.prompt: Order the immediate steps for a suspected acute hemolytic transfusion reaction:
items: Maintain IV access with normal saline
Notify blood bank; send DAT + hemolysis samples
Support BP and renal perfusion; manage DIC
cards.explain: Stop first, then resuscitate, verify, notify, and support organs.

cards.prompt: Match each transfusion reaction to its feature:
pairs: Volume overload: hypertension, raised JVP, responds to diuretic
Days-weeks later, anamnestic alloantibody, positive DAT
cards.explain: TACO is overload; TRALI is non-cardiogenic; ABO is the catastrophic immediate one.

cards.text: Anti-D antibodies are {{not}} naturally occurring; they form after exposure to D-positive cells through {{transfusion or pregnancy}}, and are prevented by {{RhD immune globulin}}.
cards.explain: Contrast with ABO antibodies, which are naturally occurring.

cards.prompt: Severe allergic/anaphylactic transfusion reactions are classically associated with which recipient factor?
cards.explain: IgA-deficient recipients can react to IgA in donor plasma, risking anaphylaxis.

cards.prompt: Match each therapy to its main use:
cards.explain: Match treatment to mechanism; never give folate alone if B12 deficiency is possible.

cards.text: Folate deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia but usually {{no}} neurologic deficit, whereas B12 deficiency can cause {{neurologic}} injury — so do not give {{folate}} alone if B12 deficiency is possible.
cards.explain: Treating with folate alone can mask anemia while neurologic B12 injury progresses.

cards.prompt: Who is the universal RBC donor and the universal RBC recipient?
cards.answer: Group O is the universal RBC donor (no A/B antigens); group AB is the universal RBC recipient (no anti-A/anti-B antibodies).

cards.text: In sickle cell disease, {{deoxygenated}} HbS {{polymerizes}}, causing sickling and leading to {{hemolysis}} and {{vaso-occlusion}}.
cards.explain: Polymerization on deoxygenation drives both hallmark processes.

cards.prompt: Match each white cell to its core function:
cards.explain: The differential localizes the type of immune problem.

cards.prompt: Order the initial workup of a microcytic anemia:
items: Ferritin + iron studies (TSAT/TIBC) and review the film
Hemoglobin electrophoresis if iron studies are normal
GI/gynecologic evaluation for the source of blood loss
cards.explain: Confirm iron status first; pursue thalassemia and the bleeding source next.

cards.prompt: Which labs fit iron deficiency anemia? (select all)
cards.explain: Iron deficiency lowers ferritin and transferrin saturation but raises TIBC and RDW.

cards.prompt: How is TACO (circulatory overload) managed and prevented?
cards.answer: Treat as volume overload — slow or divide transfusions, diurese, sit the patient up, and give oxygen as needed.

cards.prompt: When is a bone marrow biopsy especially indicated in pancytopenia?
cards.answer: When it is unexplained, severe, persistent, or associated with abnormal cells such as blasts on the film.

cards.prompt: Why is 'anemia' never an acceptable final diagnosis?
cards.answer: It is only a clue to an underlying process (loss, destruction, or underproduction); you must identify the cause.

guides.title: Week 14 · 1 CBC & the Anemia Approach
guides.html:
Foundations

CBC & the Anemia Approach

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

How to read any abnormal CBC: which cell line(s), then MCV and reticulocytes for anemia. Anemia is a clue, never a final diagnosis.

The five questions for any abnormal CBC

  • Which cell line? Red (anemia/erythrocytosis), white (leukopenia/leukocytosis), platelets (thrombocytopenia/thrombocytosis).
  • One line or many? Single line → missing raw material, loss/destruction, or a lineage problem. Bicytopenia/pancytopenia → bone-marrow (factory) problem until proven otherwise.
  • For anemia, what is the MCV? Microcytic <80, normocytic 80–100, macrocytic >100 fL.
  • What is the reticulocyte response? High → marrow responding (bleeding/hemolysis). Low/inappropriately normal → production problem.
  • What does the film show? The single most useful next test in an unexplained CBC.

MCV buckets

MCVCommon causes
MicrocyticIron deficiency, thalassemia, anemia of inflammation, sideroblastic, lead
NormocyticAcute blood loss, hemolysis, renal disease, inflammation, marrow failure, early iron deficiency
MacrocyticB12/folate deficiency, alcohol, liver disease, hypothyroidism, drugs, MDS, reticulocytosis

RDW & reticulocytes

RDW = variability of RBC size. High RDW favours iron deficiency; a normal RDW with marked microcytosis favours thalassemia trait (uniformly small cells).

Reticulocytes split loss/destruction from underproduction. MCV + reticulocyte count is the backbone of the anemia algorithm.

Anemia symptoms & signs
Fatigue, exertional dyspnea, lightheadedness, headache, conjunctival pallor, tachycardia; chest pain/ischemia, heart failure, or hypotension in severe or rapid anemia.
Anemia is never the final diagnosis — always identify the cause.

guides.title: Week 14 · 2 The Blood Film
guides.html:
Morphology

The Blood Film

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Pattern recognition on the smear — the RBC, WBC, and platelet findings that point straight to a diagnosis.

Red cell morphology that changes management

FindingThink
Microcytic / hypochromicIron deficiency, thalassemia, anemia of inflammation, sideroblastic, lead
Macro-ovalocytes + hypersegmented neutrophilsMegaloblastic anemia (B12 or folate)
Schistocytes (fragments)Microangiopathy — TTP, HUS, DIC, mechanical valve (urgent)
Spherocytes (no central pallor)Hereditary spherocytosis or autoimmune hemolysis
Target cellsThalassemia, liver disease, post-splenectomy
Sickle cellsSickle cell disease
Teardrop cells + leukoerythroblastic filmMarrow infiltration / fibrosis
PolychromasiaReticulocytosis (marrow responding)
BlastsAcute leukemia (urgent)

White cells & platelets

Neutrophils — bacterial/fungal defense; severe neutropenia is a febrile emergency. Lymphocytes — adaptive immunity. Eosinophils — allergy, parasites, drugs. Basophilia — think myeloproliferative disease (CML).

On the film, giant platelets suggest increased turnover or an inherited platelet disorder; platelet clumping causes spurious (pseudo-) thrombocytopenia.

Two film findings you never miss
Schistocytes (thrombotic microangiopathy) and blasts (acute leukemia) — both demand urgent action.

guides.title: Week 14 · 3 Oxygen Delivery & Hemoglobin
guides.html:
Physiology

Oxygen Delivery & Hemoglobin

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

How hemoglobin carries and releases oxygen — cooperative binding, the sigmoid curve, and what shifts it.

Hemoglobin structure

Adult HbA = 2α + 2β chains, each of the 4 globin chains carrying a heme with iron that binds one O₂ — so one Hb binds up to 4 O₂ molecules. Fetal HbF = 2α + 2γ, with higher O₂ affinity so the fetus extracts O₂ across the placenta.

Cooperative binding & the curve

Binding of one O₂ raises affinity for the next → the classic sigmoidal O₂–dissociation curve.

ShiftAffinity / effectCauses
RightLower affinity → more unloading to tissue↑CO₂, ↑H⁺/low pH, ↑temperature, ↑2,3-BPG, exercise
LeftHigher affinity → less unloading↓CO₂, alkalosis, ↓temperature, ↓2,3-BPG, HbF, carbon monoxide
Bohr effect
Metabolically active tissue makes CO₂ and H⁺, which lower Hb affinity, shift the curve right, and promote O₂ unloading exactly where it is needed.
Carbon monoxide
CO binds Hb with ~250× the affinity of O₂, shifts the curve left, and gives a falsely normal pulse oximetry reading — suspect it clinically and measure carboxyhemoglobin.

guides.title: Week 14 · 4 Hematopoiesis & Pancytopenia
guides.html:
Marrow

Hematopoiesis & Pancytopenia

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Where blood is made, and how to think about the patient whose marrow factory is failing.

Hematopoiesis

Mature blood cells arise from hematopoietic stem cells in marrow: self-renewal, commitment, proliferation, maturation.

StageMain site
Early embryoYolk sac
FetusFetal liver → then marrow
AdultAxial skeleton: pelvis, vertebrae, ribs, sternum, proximal long bones

Pancytopenia = all three lines down

Anemia + leukopenia/neutropenia + thrombocytopenia. Usually a marrow problem — failure, replacement, or ineffective hematopoiesis — until proven otherwise.

CategoryExampleClue
Marrow failureAplastic anemiaHypocellular marrow, no splenomegaly
Infiltration / replacementLeukemia, lymphoma, mets, myelofibrosisBlasts, teardrops, leukoerythroblastic film
Ineffective hematopoiesisB12/folate deficiency, MDSMacrocytosis, hypersegmented neutrophils
Destruction / sequestrationHypersplenism, autoimmuneSplenomegaly
Infection / drugs / toxinsHIV, EBV, chemo, alcoholExposure history
Urgent red flags
Febrile neutropenia · platelets <10 or active bleeding · blasts on smear · severe symptomatic anemia · rapidly falling counts · schistocytes/neuro/renal failure (TTP/HUS).

Work-up: confirm on repeat CBC, review differential + ANC, urgent film, reticulocytes, screen reversible causes (B12/folate, iron, liver, renal, viral); marrow biopsy when unexplained, severe, or with abnormal cells.

guides.title: Week 14 · 5 Iron Deficiency Anemia
guides.html:
Anemia

Iron Deficiency Anemia

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

The most common anemia worldwide: stages, the lab pattern, the ferritin trap, treatment, and finding the cause.

Stages & lab pattern

Depleted stores (low ferritin, normal CBC) → iron-restricted erythropoiesis (low TSAT, high TIBC) → iron deficiency anemia (low Hb, low MCV, high RDW, microcytic hypochromic film).

TestIron deficiency
FerritinLow (best single test for stores)
Serum iron / TSATLow
TIBC / transferrinHigh
RDWHigh
PlateletsNormal or reactive thrombocytosis
The ferritin trap
Ferritin is an acute-phase reactant. Low ferritin is diagnostic of iron deficiency, but a normal/high ferritin does not exclude it in inflammation — check transferrin saturation/TIBC.

Iron deficiency vs thalassemia trait

FeatureIron deficiencyThalassemia trait
RDWHighNormal / mildly up
RBC countLow/normalNormal/high for the Hb
FerritinLowNormal/high
ElectrophoresisNormalβ-trait: ↑HbA₂

Treatment & cause

Two goals: replace iron and treat the cause. Oral iron (ferrous sulfate/fumarate/gluconate), often alternate-day; away from calcium/antacids/tea; continue after Hb normalizes to refill stores. IV iron if intolerant, malabsorption, ongoing loss, or rapid repletion needed. Transfusion only for instability, major bleeding, or tissue hypoxia — it does not correct the deficiency.

New iron deficiency in a man or postmenopausal woman warrants evaluation for GI blood loss / malignancy until proven otherwise.

guides.title: Week 14 · 6 Thalassemia & Sickle Cell
guides.html:
Hemoglobinopathies

Thalassemia & Sickle Cell

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Two inherited routes to anemia: too little globin (thalassemia) and abnormal globin (sickle). Selected for by malaria.

Thalassemia — reduced globin production

Reduced α or β chains → imbalanced synthesis damages precursors → microcytosis ± hemolysis. Severity ranges from silent carrier → trait (mild anemia, very low MCV, ↑HbA₂ in β-trait) → intermedia → major (transfusion-dependent, marrow expansion).

Do not give iron blindly
Microcytosis is not always iron deficiency. In thalassemia, do not prescribe iron unless deficiency is confirmed — iron loading is a real risk.

Sickle cell disease

A structural β-globin mutation makes HbS; deoxygenated HbS polymerizes → sickling → hemolysis + vaso-occlusion.

ComplicationNote
Vaso-occlusive crisisPrompt analgesia (often opioids), hydration, treat triggers, O₂ only if hypoxic
Acute chest syndromeChest pain + fever + hypoxia + new infiltrate; O₂, antibiotics, analgesia, ± (exchange) transfusion (emergency)
Functional aspleniaRisk from encapsulated organisms → vaccination + penicillin prophylaxis in children
OthersStroke, splenic sequestration, dactylitis, priapism, avascular necrosis, CKD, gallstones
Disease-modifying therapy
Hydroxyurea raises HbF and reduces vaso-occlusive episodes (monitor CBC for myelosuppression). Folic acid supports erythropoiesis; HSCT is curative in selected patients.

guides.title: Week 14 · 7 Thrombocytopenia & Bleeding
guides.html:
Hemostasis

Thrombocytopenia & Bleeding

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Reading the bleeding pattern, the causes that matter, and the counts and film findings that signal an emergency.

Bleeding pattern localizes the defect

Platelet / primary hemostasisCoagulation factor / secondary
Petechiae, purpuraDeep tissue bleeds
Mucosal bleeding, epistaxis, gum bleedingHemarthroses, large hematomas
MenorrhagiaDelayed bleeding after procedures

Causes that change management

CauseCluePrinciple
ITPIsolated thrombocytopenia, otherwise normal CBC/filmSteroids/IVIG if severe; observe mild cases
TTPThrombocytopenia + schistocytes + neuro/renal ± feverEmergency plasma exchange; avoid platelet transfusion
DICSepsis/trauma/obstetric; ↑PT/PTT, ↓fibrinogen, ↑D-dimer, schistocytesTreat the cause; product support
Marrow failure / leukemiaOther cytopenias, blastsUrgent hematology + marrow
Drug-induced / hypersplenismNew drug; splenomegaly/liver diseaseStop culprit; treat underlying

Platelet thresholds

  • <50 ×10⁹/L — bleeding risk with procedures/trauma
  • <20 ×10⁹/L — higher spontaneous risk
  • <10 ×10⁹/L — significant risk of spontaneous serious bleeding
Film red flags
Schistocytes → TTP/HUS/DIC. Blasts → acute leukemia. Both urgent. Always exclude pseudothrombocytopenia from clumping first.

guides.title: Week 14 · 8 Blood Groups & Transfusion
guides.html:
Transfusion

Blood Groups & Transfusion

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

ABO and Rh, the catastrophic ABO mismatch, the named transfusion reactions, and preventing hemolytic disease of the newborn.

ABO system

GroupRBC antigensPlasma antibodiesCan receive RBCs from
AAAnti-BA, O
BBAnti-AB, O
ABA + BNoneA, B, AB, O (universal RBC recipient)
ONoneAnti-A + Anti-BO only (universal RBC donor)
Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (ABO mismatch)
Recipient IgM binds donor RBC antigens → complement → intravascular hemolysis: fever, flank/back pain, hypotension, hemoglobinuria, DIC, AKI. Stop the transfusion, saline, clerical check, notify blood bank, send DAT/hemolysis labs, support BP and kidneys.

Named reactions

ReactionFeatures
Febrile non-hemolyticFever/chills, no hemolysis
Allergic / anaphylaxisUrticaria → anaphylaxis; severe in IgA deficiency
TACOCirculatory overload: dyspnea, hypertension, pulmonary edema, ↑BNP, responds to diuretic
TRALIAcute hypoxemia + bilateral infiltrates within 6 h, non-cardiogenic, normal volume status
Delayed hemolyticDays–weeks later; anamnestic alloantibody (e.g. Kidd); falling Hb, positive DAT

Rh & hemolytic disease of the newborn

Anti-D is not naturally occurring — it forms after exposure to D-positive RBCs (transfusion or pregnancy). An RhD-negative mother sensitized to a D-positive fetus can make anti-D IgG that crosses the placenta in a later pregnancy → fetal hemolysis.

Prevention
RhD immune globulin given to appropriate RhD-negative patients passively clears fetal D-positive cells before the maternal immune system responds, preventing anti-D alloimmunization.
